Okay, so this is a video that I'm super excited to make for you guys. What is your gym's max potential? And when you answer this question from a mathematical standpoint, you can see exactly where you should be putting your effort, okay? So the equation that you can use to calculate what I call the burn rate, but basically you can calculate a gym's theoretical recurring revenue based on two numbers. One is, how many sign ups are you averaging each month? And the second is your attrition percentage. And what I mean by that is, let's say 10% of your clients leave, okay, every month and that's just your, that's your turn, right? The example, and if you want to use this into a more useful mathematical equation, then it's going to be the number of sign ups you have over the percentage of people who leave your gym, and that will give you your max capacity at your gym. So here's some numbers. Let's say that you on average sign up 10 people per month, okay? And it's on average 10% of your clients per month leave, which means, you know, between and it's not just like people who cancel, but a lot of them are going to be declines that never call you back if you know what I'm talking about. And usually those are like three quarters of them. Now people who like actually are like, I hate you. I never want to see you again. They usually just fizzle out of the darkness and let their card decline and then that's it, right? So in that example, 10% of people who you have at your gym leave on a month to month basis and you sign up 10 people, notice 10% versus 10 people, that gives you 100 person max cap. Now that should answer this question. Why is it so easy in the beginning for people to grow their gyms? Like I would say 90% of the gyms that I talked to have about 80 clients, 80 to 100 clients. And that's because the two numbers because because of these two numbers, right? And so if you want to grow your gym, then you want this one to be high and you want this one to be low, right? You want your attrition percentage to be as low as humanly possible. Now here's something that's really cool. Let's say that you kept your 10 sign ups, okay? 10 sign ups a month, you didn't change that. And then you dropped your term percentage or your attrition percentage by 5%. Okay? Now it's deceiving when I say it this way because a lot of like, you know, attention experts will be like, oh, you have to do this drop by 5%. That's a 50% drop. Okay? That's, you need to think about that. But let me just illustrate with the math though. You still keep your same sign ups and you drop your attrition rate to 5%. That overnight increases your capacity at your gym to 200 people. How big of a difference is that? Enormous, right? Another 100 people at whatever your price point is. Like that's the difference usually between most guys breaking even and losing money and making a healthy profit. Okay? So there's obviously two sides of the equation. Now here's something that's also cool. Let's double your sign ups. Okay? And we keep your percentage at 10%. Right? Then what's the number there? Again, it's going to be 200 max. Okay? Hopefully you can see that. All right? Because 200 divided by 0.1 is going to be 200. Okay? Now that's like, this is where most of the gyms that I talk to, if they are at 200, it's usually because of these numbers. They usually focus on the sign ups. But the thing is that it's a whole lot cheaper, believe it or not, to manage attrition, but most people just don't know how. Okay? And that's a lot of the stuff that we cover in creating an absolutely amazing customer experience, sending regular things just because to make sure that your clients just feel loved, they're appreciated, giving surprise gifts, and then having a customer service training that's reinforced on a weekly basis for how to handle customer interactions to maximize their experience. Okay? So this is the math behind figuring out why your gym's at. Now if you're like, I just can't seem to get past this point, it's because of this. And so it means that one of these two numbers has to change. Either your sign ups have to go up or your attrition has to go down. And that's really all it comes down to. And so all you have to do then is just focus on those two numbers. Makes it pretty simple, right? So anyways, hope this is useful for you. This is how you can literally understand your business a little bit better so you know where your actual max capacity is, not what you want it to be. I want to have four under customers. Well, if you have a 10% insurance, then it means that you have to sign up 10 new EFTs per week. Now some people can do that because they have strong enough marketing, but a lot of people can't. Okay? And so that's where this stuff comes in handy and starting to attack both sides of the equation. Because even if you want to look at another realistic number, let's just say you increase this to 15 and decrease this to 7.5. Okay? These are huge, huge increases, right? But you're going to have about the same result. I'm pretty sure that's 200. Again, but adding an extra one person a week on top of what you're normally signing up and then just losing, you know, two and a half or like really losing one less person every other week can make an enormous difference in your business. So where you'd be losing one less person per week. So hope that was useful. Hope you enjoyed it as much as I enjoyed making it for you. And I'll see you on the other side.
